label: Elastic APM
description: Connect to Elastic APM to ingest traces, spans, transactions, and error data from your applications via the APM Server.

form.title: Connect Elastic APM
form.subtitle: Point Anton at your APM Server and supply a secret token or API key to start ingesting traces and performance data.

methods.label: Secret Token
methods.description: Paste your APM Server URL and the secret token configured in Kibana. The simplest and most common auth method for self-managed and Elastic Cloud APM deployments.
methods.how_to:
## Connect with APM Server Secret Token

This is the default authentication method for Elastic APM Server. You'll find both values in Kibana.

### Steps

1. Open **Kibana** and navigate to **Stack Management → Fleet → Agent Policies** (or directly to **Observability → APM**).
2. Click your APM integration or agent policy to view its settings.
3. Under **APM Server**, copy the **Server URL** (e.g. `https://your-cluster.apm.us-east-1.aws.elastic-cloud.com:443`).
4. In the same section, locate the **Secret Token** field. If it is masked, click the eye icon or **Reveal** to copy it.
   - On self-managed clusters the token is also visible in `apm-server.yml` under `apm-server.auth.secret_token`.
5. Paste both values into the fields below and click **Submit**.

### Notes

- Secret tokens are shared secrets — treat them like passwords. Rotate them in Fleet whenever you suspect exposure.
- If your APM Server is behind a custom TLS certificate you may need to supply the CA certificate.
- On Elastic Cloud the URL always includes the port; don't strip it.

fields.name: apm_server_url
fields.label: APM Server URL
fields.description: Full URL of your APM Server, including the port. Found in Kibana → Stack Management → Fleet → APM Integration.

fields.name: secret_token
fields.label: Secret Token
fields.description: The shared secret token configured on the APM Server. Found in Kibana → Stack Management → Fleet → APM Integration → APM Server settings.

fields.name: ca_cert
fields.label: CA Certificate (optional)
fields.description: PEM-encoded CA certificate. Only needed if your APM Server uses a self-signed or private TLS certificate.

methods.label: API Key
methods.description: Use an Elastic API key instead of a secret token. Supports fine-grained permissions and is the recommended method when multiple services connect to the same APM Server.
methods.how_to:
## Connect with an Elastic API Key

API keys let you grant scoped, revocable credentials without sharing the cluster's master secret token.

### Steps

1. Open **Kibana** and navigate to **Stack Management → Fleet → Agent Policies** and locate your APM integration to copy the **Server URL**.
2. To create an API key, go to **Stack Management → Security → API Keys**.
3. Click **Create API key**. Give it a descriptive name like `Anton APM`.
4. Under **Privileges**, grant at minimum `apm` ingest permissions. For read access you may also need `monitor` on the APM indices.
5. Click **Create API key**. Copy the generated key — it is shown **only once**.
6. Paste the APM Server URL and API key into the fields below and click **Submit**.

### Notes

- The API key format is `id:api_key` (base64-encoded). Paste it exactly as shown after creation.
- API keys can be individually revoked from **Stack Management → Security → API Keys** without affecting other integrations.
- Elastic Cloud users can also generate API keys from the Elastic Cloud console under the deployment's **Security** tab.

fields.name: apm_server_url
fields.label: APM Server URL
fields.description: Full URL of your APM Server, including the port. Found in Kibana → Stack Management → Fleet → APM Integration.

fields.name: api_key
fields.label: API Key
fields.description: Elastic API key in `id:key` format. Generated in Kibana → Stack Management → Security → API Keys. Shown only once at creation.

fields.name: ca_cert
fields.label: CA Certificate (optional)
fields.description: PEM-encoded CA certificate. Only needed if your APM Server uses a self-signed or private TLS certificate.

methods.label: No Authentication (Anonymous)
methods.description: Connect to an APM Server that has authentication disabled. Only suitable for local development or tightly network-controlled environments.
methods.how_to:
## Connect to an Unauthenticated APM Server

Some local development or internal APM Server deployments disable authentication entirely. Use this method only in trusted, non-production environments.

### Steps

1. Confirm your APM Server has authentication disabled. In `apm-server.yml` this looks like `apm-server.auth.anonymous.enabled: true` or no auth block at all.
2. Copy the APM Server URL (e.g. `http://localhost:8200`).
3. Paste it into the field below and click **Submit**.

### Notes

- Do not use this method for production deployments. Without auth, any process that can reach the APM Server can send data.
- Elastic Cloud always enforces authentication — this method is not compatible with Elastic Cloud deployments.

fields.name: apm_server_url
fields.label: APM Server URL
fields.description: Full URL of your APM Server. For local development this is typically http://localhost:8200.
